---
title: "GIS"
author: "Uzair Hendricks"
date: "2026-02-25"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r,message=FALSE}
##this contains work for my GIS project to help visualize the temperature range that white-browed sparrow-weavers inhabit. 

#prepping packages

library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggspatial)
library(rinat)
library(ggplot2)
install.packages("prettymapr")
```

```{r}
#reading in the data from iNaturalist for the white-browed sparrow weaver

bird = get_inat_obs(taxon_name = "Plocepasser mahali",
                    bounds = c(-35, -18,-22,33),
                    maxresults = 2000)

#read first few rows of the data

head(bird)


```
```{r}
#filtering the data by a range of column atribute criteria

bird <- bird %>% filter(positional_accuracy<10000 & 
                latitude<0 &
                !is.na(latitude) &
                captive_cultivated == "false" &
                quality_grade == "research")

class(bird)
```
```{r}
#making the dataframe into a class sf spatial object

bird <- st_as_sf(bird, coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 4326)

#checking what class it is

class(bird)

#checking for new geometry column

names(bird)
```
```{r}
#plotting out the data points

ggplot() + geom_sf(data=bird)
```

```{r}
#adding the basemap to the plot

library(rosm)
library(ggspatial)

ggplot() + 
  annotation_map_tile(type = "osm", progress = "none")+
  geom_sf(data=bird)

```

```{r}
####creating an interactive map

library(leaflet)
library(htmltools)
leaflet() %>% 
  # add default OpenStreetMap tiles
  addTiles(group = "Default") %>%
  
  #Add my points
  
  addCircleMarkers(data=bird,
                   group = "Plocepasser mahali",
                   radius = 2,
                   color = "brown")

#checking the map datapoints

library(mapview)
library(leafpop)

mapview(bird,
        popup = 
          popupTable(bird,
                     zcol = c("user_login", "captive_cultivated", "url")))

#making the URL's live links

Live_bird <- bird %>%
  mutate(click_url = paste("<b><a href='", url, "'>Link to iNat observation</a></b>"))

mapview(bird, 
        popup = 
          popupTable(Live_bird,
            zcol = c("user_login", "captive_cultivated", "click_url")))
```

```{r, message=FALSE}
#getting global temperature data

library(geodata)

temp = worldclim_global(var = "tavg", res = 5, path = tempdir())

#cropping global temp data to Southern Africa

box = ext(10, 35, -35, -22)
temp = crop(temp, box)


library(terra)

#checking the crs of temp data

crs(temp)

#reprojecting the temp data

tproj = project(temp, crs(bird), method = "bilinear")
crs(tproj)
ncell(tproj)
```

```{r}

#plotting the temperature data

plot(tproj[[1]], main = "Average Temperature")
```
```{r}
# Ensure the bird data matches the raster's map projection
bird_proj <- st_transform(bird, crs(tproj))

#converting temperature raster data to a data frame

tempdf <- as.data.frame(tproj[[1]], xy = TRUE)

#renaming temperature column for fill argument

names(tempdf)[3] <- "Temp"

#plottting

# 2. Plotting
ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = tempdf, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = Temp)) +
  geom_sf(data = bird, colour = "black", size = 1) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Avg Temp (°C)") +
  labs(title = "Plocepasser mahali Distribution vs Temperature") +
  theme_minimal()
```


```{r}
leaflet() %>% 
  # add default OpenStreetMap tiles
  addTiles(group = "Default") %>%
  
  #Add my points
  
  addCircleMarkers(data=tproj,
                   radius = 2,
                   color = "brown")
```
